figures.caption: Table 2: Deception awareness scale used by the LLM judge to score reasoning traces. Only instances where the agent deviated from its announcement are scored.
Table 29: Full deception awareness score distributions for each model and group size. Scores are assigned by GPT-5.1 as judge on a 1–5 scale.
Table 4: Overall lying rates for n = 3.
Table 5: Overall lying rates for n = 4.
Table 3: Overall lying rates by model and group size, averaged across games. Rates are computed over majority-vote decisions (five runs per scenario).
Table 25: Consensus statistics by model, sorted by average consensus rate. The 5/5, 4/5, 3/5, and ≤2/5 columns report the percentage of scenarios at each agreement level.

Table 24: Number of scenarios (canonical announcement profiles) per game and group size. Each scenario tests one focal agent against aggregate opponent information. Symmetry reduction exploits player interchangeability in symmetric games, reducing the search space by 97% for 5-agent continuous games (from 65 = 7,776 to 252 canonical profiles). The full evaluation comprises 6,735 LLM queries per model, with majority vote over 5 samples determining each decision.

Figure 1: Evaluation framework: Scenario generation selects games and algorithmically enumerates promise-breaking opportunities. Behavioral evaluation queries nine frontier LLMs, classifies deviations, and scores reasoning traces for deception awareness.
Figure 2: Opportunity-based exploitation rates by behavioral quadrant, averaged across games and group sizes. Each rate is conditioned on the relevant opportunity type existing.

Table 26: Consensus statistics by game and group size. Binary-action games (Volunteer’s Dilemma, Diner’s Dilemma, El Farol Bar) show higher consensus than numerical-action games (Tragedy of Commons, Public Goods, Weakest Link), reflecting the larger action space in the latter.
Table 16: Altruistic lying rates for n = 3: lying when payoff decreases but collective welfare improves.
Table 15: Missed opportunity rates for n = 5: did not lie when a win-win deviation was available.
Table 14: Missed opportunity rates for n = 4: did not lie when a win-win deviation was available.
Table 17: Altruistic lying rates for n = 4: lying when payoff decreases but collective welfare improves.

Figure 4: Model characterization in the profitability–prosociality space. Each point represents a model, with the x-coordinate measuring the fraction of lies that are individually profitable and the y-coordinate measuring the fraction that are prosocial. Most models fall in the win-win quadrant (high x, high y).

Figure 3: Missed opportunity rates by model, averaged across group sizes. Missed opportunities are concentrated primarily in the Weakest Link Game, with moderate contributions from Tragedy of Commons and El Farol.
Table 1: Six canonical games spanning binary and numerical action spaces.
Table 18: Altruistic lying rates for n = 5: lying when payoff decreases but collective welfare improves.
Table 21: Sabotaging rates for n = 5: lying when both individual payoff decreases and collective welfare worsens.
Table 19: Sabotaging rates for n = 3: lying when both individual payoff decreases and collective welfare worsens.
Table 20: Sabotaging rates for n = 4: lying when both individual payoff decreases and collective welfare worsens.

Table 13: Missed opportunity rates for n = 3: did not lie when a win-win deviation was available.
Table 10: Selfish lying rates for n = 3: lying when payoff increases but collective welfare worsens.
Table 12: Selfish lying rates for n = 5: lying when payoff increases but collective welfare worsens.
Table 23: Collective welfare metrics by game. Each metric is used to compute the collective welfare change σi that determines whether a lie is classified as prosocial or antisocial.
Table 22: Formal game specifications. All games are symmetric, one-shot, and payoffdeterministic. Group sizes evaluated: n ∈ {3, 4, 5}.
Table 7: Win-Win lying rates for n = 3: lying when payoff increases and collective welfare does not worsen.
